prompt:
cidrkit does parse + Contains today and that was enough until the firewall-rule compiler started emitting 4000-line allowlists. Three ops needed, all pure address math on the existing Net type. rangeops_test.go is the contract; read it before arguing with me about canonical forms.

Merge(nets []Net) []Net — collapse a pile of blocks into the minimal set covering exactly the same addresses. Overlaps and duplicates go away, adjacent aligned siblings fuse into the parent (recursively — /25 + two /26s that line up must come out as one /24), adjacent-but-unaligned blocks must NOT fuse into something bigger than the input. Output sorted by network address.

Net.Split(parts int) ([]Net, error) — cut a block into that many equal subnets, in address order. parts has to be a positive power of two, and the block has to be big enough; anything else is an error, not a best-effort. Split(1) is the block itself.

Exclude(outer Net, holes []Net) []Net — everything in outer not covered by any hole, again as the minimal sorted CIDR set. Holes may overlap each other, poke out past outer, or miss it entirely; only the intersection matters. Holes covering all of outer means an empty result.

Do NOT grow new representations — parse/format/Contains behavior is pinned by cidr_test.go and half our tools link against it. Both test files stay untouched. Green means: go test -race -timeout 30s ./...
